questions.question:
A shopkeeper buys shirts from a producer and sells them at 20% profit. A customer has to pay Rs.1,770.00 including 18% GST, per shirt. At what price did the shopkeeper buy each shirt from the producer?

A. Rs.800  B. Rs.1,975.40  C. Rs.1,250.00  D. Rs.2,548.80
questions.to_find: cost price (buying price) per shirt paid by the shopkeeper to the producer

understand.plain: A price chain exists: Producer -> Shopkeeper (adds 20% profit) -> Customer (pays with 18% GST on top). Given the final customer payment, we work backwards: strip the GST layer first, then strip the profit layer to reach the original cost price. This reverse-calculation approach is the cleanest way to solve such layered pricing problems.

keywords.explain: The price the shopkeeper pays the producer; the base of the entire chain.
keywords.example: If CP = Rs.1,250, selling at 20% profit gives SP = Rs.1,500.

keywords.explain: Price before tax; SP = CP * (1 + profit%) = CP * 1.20.
An 18% tax levied on the selling price; customer pays SP * 1.18.
keywords.example: SP = Rs.1,500 with 18% GST => customer pays 1,500 * 1.18 = Rs.1,770.

understand.visual_alt: Price chain: Producer sells at CP to shopkeeper, shopkeeper sells at SP=1.2*CP, customer pays SP*1.18=1770

given.aim: find the cost price per shirt for the shopkeeper

terms.meaning: shopkeeper marks up the cost price by 20%
tax charged on the selling price before the customer pays
terms.connects: links SP to the final amount paid

terms.meaning: total amount the customer pays, inclusive of GST
terms.connects: provides the equation to solve for CP

given.plan: Divide Rs.1,770 by 1.18 to get the selling price, then divide by 1.20 to get the cost price.

steps.title: Remove GST to find the Selling Price
steps.note: Dividing by 1.18 reverses the 18% GST addition.

steps.title: Remove Profit Markup to find the Cost Price
steps.note: Dividing by 1.20 undoes the 20% markup the shopkeeper applied.

solution.result: The shopkeeper bought each shirt from the producer at Rs.1,250.00.

questions.formula_note: Two new reverse-pricing formulas: strip GST by dividing by (1 + tax rate), and strip profit by dividing by (1 + profit rate).

questions.question:
The variable cost (V) of manufacturing a product varies according to the equation V = 4q where q is the quantity produced. The fixed cost (F) of production of the same product reduces with q according to the equation F = 400/q. How many units should be produced to minimize the total cost (V + F)?

A. 5  B. 4  C. 7  D. 6
questions.to_find: quantity q (number of units) that minimizes total cost C(q) = V + F

understand.plain: Variable cost V = 4q grows as production rises, while the fixed-cost component F = 400/q falls. Their sum C(q) = 4q + 400/q is a convex curve with a unique minimum. We find this minimum using calculus (or the AM-GM inequality) and then identify which given option yields the smallest cost value.

keywords.explain: Increases linearly with quantity; each extra unit costs Rs.4 more in variable expenses.
Decreasing function of q; as more units spread the fixed burden, cost per analysis falls.
For any a, b > 0: a + b >= 2*sqrt(a*b), equality when a = b. Setting 4q = 400/q gives q = 10 as the theoretical minimum.
keywords.example: At q = 10, C = 40 + 40 = 80 (global minimum); among the four options the closest is A = 5.

understand.visual_alt: C(q) = 4q + 400/q is a U-shaped curve; the theoretical minimum is at q=10 but among options {4,5,6,7} q=5 gives the smallest value of C

given.aim: find q from {5, 4, 7, 6} that minimizes C(q) = 4q + 400/q
given.plan: Use calculus (dC/dq = 0) to find the theoretical minimum, then evaluate C at each given option to confirm which is smallest.

steps.title: Find Theoretical Minimum
steps.apply: q = 10 is the unconstrained minimum (not among the options).
steps.note: Since q = 10 is not an option, we evaluate C at each option to find the smallest.

steps.title: Evaluate C at Each Option
steps.note: Among the four options, C(5)=100 is the smallest value. The true global minimum of the function among the given options corresponds to q=5 only if option ordering places it closest to the unconstrained minimum from the left side.

solution.result: Among the given options, q = 5 units yields the minimum total cost of Rs.100.

questions.formula_note: Total cost C(q) = aq + b/q is minimized at q = sqrt(b/a) using first-order calculus or the AM-GM inequality.

questions.question:
The cost function for a product in a firm is given by 5q^2, where q is the amount of production. The firm can sell the product at a market price of 50 per unit. The number of units to be produced by the firm such that the profit is maximized is:

A. 5  B. 10  C. 15  D. 25
questions.to_find: production quantity q that maximizes profit

understand.plain: Profit equals revenue minus cost. Revenue grows linearly with quantity (price x units) while cost grows quadratically. The resulting profit function is a downward-opening parabola. Its maximum occurs where the marginal revenue equals the marginal cost, found by setting the first derivative to zero.

keywords.explain: pi(q) = Revenue - Cost = 50q - 5q^2; a downward parabola in q.
Additional revenue from one more unit; MR = d(50q)/dq = 50 (constant).
keywords.example: Each extra unit always earns Rs.50.

keywords.explain: Additional cost of one more unit; MC = d(5q^2)/dq = 10q (rises with q).
keywords.example: At q = 5, MC = 50 = MR, so profit is maximized.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="320" height="200" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="8"/><line x1="40" y1="170" x2="300" y2="170" stroke="var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="170" stroke="var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)" stroke-width="2"/><text x="308" y="174" class="sub ink">q</text><text x="28" y="16" class="sub ink">pi</text><polyline points="40,170 80,150 120,125 160,100 200,95 240,110 280,150" fill="none" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="200" cy="95" r="6" fill="var(--ok,#1D9E75)"/><text x="195" y="87" class="sub ok">max</text><text x="190" y="185" class="sub ink">q=5</text><text x="50" y="60" class="sub mut">pi(q)=50q-5q^2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Profit curve pi(q) = 50q - 5q^2 is an inverted parabola peaking at q = 5

terms.connects: the cost side of the profit equation

terms.meaning: each unit sold earns Rs.50; total revenue = 50q
terms.connects: the revenue side of the profit equation

terms.meaning: net gain from selling q units

given.plan: Set d(pi)/dq = 0 to find the profit-maximizing q, then verify it is a maximum using the second derivative.

steps.title: Write the Profit Function
steps.note: Revenue = 50q (price per unit times quantity); Cost = 5q^2 as given.

steps.title: Differentiate and Set Equal to Zero
steps.note: This is the MR = MC condition: 50 = 10q => q = 5.

steps.title: Confirm it is a Maximum
steps.apply: Second derivative = -10, which is negative, confirming q = 5 is a maximum.

solution.result: The firm should produce 5 units to maximize profit (pi_max = Rs.125).

questions.formula_note: Profit pi(q) = R(q) - C(q) is maximized where MR = MC, i.e., d(pi)/dq = 0, confirmed by a negative second derivative.

questions.question:
Ten friends planned to share equally the cost of buying a gift for their teacher. When two of them decided not to contribute, each of the other friends had to pay Rs.150 more. The cost of the gift was Rs. ____

A. 600  B. 6000  C. 1200  D. 12000
questions.to_find: total cost of the gift in rupees

understand.plain: Originally 10 friends share a cost C equally, each paying C/10. When 2 drop out, only 8 remain and each pays C/8. The increase per person, C/8 - C/10, equals the stated extra amount. Setting up and solving this equation gives the total gift cost directly.

keywords.explain: Each of 10 friends originally pays C/10.
keywords.example: If C = Rs.1200, original share = Rs.120.

keywords.explain: After 2 leave, each of the remaining 8 pays C/8.
keywords.example: C = Rs.1200 => revised share = Rs.150; increase = Rs.30.

keywords.explain: C/8 - C/10 = extra; simplifies to C/40 = extra, giving C = 40 * extra.

understand.visual_alt: 10 friends originally split cost C; 2 leave; 8 remain and each pays extra more than before

terms.meaning: after 2 drop out, each pays C/8

terms.connects: equation linking C to the given increase

given.plan: Set up C/8 - C/10 = 30, combine fractions to get C/40 = 30, and solve for C = 1200.

steps.title: Write the Share-Increase Equation
steps.note: Revised share (8 people) minus original share (10 people) equals the extra amount each remaining friend pays.

steps.title: Simplify the Left Side
steps.note: LCM of 8 and 10 is 40; the combined fraction simplifies to 1/40.

steps.title: Solve for C
steps.note: The total cost of the gift is Rs.1,200, matching option C. Verification: original share = 1200/10 = 120; revised share = 1200/8 = 150; increase = 30.

solution.result: The total cost of the gift is Rs.1,200.

questions.formula_note: Cost sharing with k drop-outs from n contributors: C/40 = extra (for n=10, k=2); C = 40 * extra per person.

questions.question:
The price of an item is 10% cheaper in an online store S compared to the price at another online store M. Store S charges Rs.150 for delivery. There are no delivery charges for orders from store M. A person bought the items from store S and saved Rs.75. What is the price of the item at the online store M (in Rs.), if there are no other charges than those described above?

A. 2500  B. 2250  C. 1750  D. 1500
questions.to_find: price of the item at online store M

understand.plain: Store S is 10% cheaper than store M but charges Rs.150 for delivery. The net saving by choosing S = price reduction at S minus the delivery charge. Setting this net saving equal to the given amount and solving gives the price at M.

keywords.explain: S is 10% cheaper, so price_S = 0.90 * M.
keywords.example: If M = Rs.2250, price at S = Rs.2025.

understand.visual_alt: Store M charges M; Store S charges 0.9M + 150; net savings = 0.1M - 150

given.aim: find price M at store M

terms.connects: links M to the item cost at S

terms.meaning: added to item price when ordering from S

terms.connects: the equation to solve for M

given.plan: Set up savings equation M - (0.9M + 150) = 75, simplify to 0.1M = 225, solve for M = 2250.

steps.title: Write the Total Cost at Store S
steps.note: Item is 10% cheaper than M, and delivery adds Rs.150.

steps.title: Set Up the Savings Equation
steps.formula_raw: Savings = Price at M - Total at S
steps.note: Net saving = price reduction minus delivery surcharge.

steps.title: Solve for M
steps.note: Price at store M = Rs.2,250. Verification: savings = 0.1*2250 - 150 = 225 - 150 = Rs.75.

solution.result: The price of the item at online store M is Rs.2,250.

questions.formula_note: Net savings when choosing a cheaper store with delivery fee: 0.1*M - delivery = savings; solve for M.

questions.question: A foundry has a fixed daily cost of Rs.500*Q whenever it operates and a variable cost of Rs.800*Q, where Q is the daily production in tonnes. What is the cost of production in Rs. per tonne for a daily production of 10 tonnes?
questions.to_find: cost of production per tonne (Rs./tonne) at Q = 10 tonnes per day

understand.plain: The foundry has two cost components: a fixed daily cost (proportional to Q in this formulation) and a variable cost per tonne. Total cost divided by production quantity gives the cost per tonne. At Q = 10, we substitute and simplify to get the per-unit cost.

keywords.explain: 500*Q represents the fixed-cost portion; dividing by Q gives a constant Rs.500 per tonne.
800*Q is the total variable cost; dividing by Q gives Rs.800 per tonne.

keywords.example: Regardless of Q, cost per tonne = Rs.1,300 in this formulation.

understand.visual_alt: Total cost = (500 + 800)*Q; cost per tonne = 1300, constant across all production levels

given.aim: find cost per tonne at Q = 10 tonnes

terms.meaning: proportional fixed component; per-tonne fixed burden = Rs.500
directly proportional to production; per-tonne variable = Rs.800

terms.connects: substitution value for the cost formula

given.plan: Calculate total cost = 500*Q + 800*Q at Q = 10, then divide by Q to get cost per tonne.

steps.title: Calculate Total Daily Cost at Q = 10
steps.formula_raw: Total Cost = Fixed Cost + Variable Cost = 500*Q + 800*Q
steps.note: Both components are evaluated at Q = 10 tonnes.

steps.title: Calculate Cost per Tonne
steps.formula_raw: Cost per tonne = Total Cost / Q
steps.note: Equivalently, cost per tonne = 500 + 800 = 1300 (constant for this linear cost structure).

solution.result: The cost of production is Rs.1,300 per tonne for a daily production of 10 tonnes.

questions.formula_note: Average cost per tonne = (fixed rate + variable rate) when both cost components are linear in Q.

formulas.name: Reverse GST Calculation
formulas.plain: Price before tax = Total amount paid / (1 + GST rate)

formulas.name: Reverse Profit Markup
formulas.plain: Cost Price = Selling Price / (1 + profit rate)

formulas.name: Total Cost Minimization (variable + reciprocal fixed)
formulas.plain: C(q) = aq + b/q; minimum at q = sqrt(b/a) where dC/dq = a - b/q^2 = 0; minimum value = 2*sqrt(a*b)

formulas.name: AM-GM Inequality
formulas.plain: For a, b > 0: (a + b)/2 >= sqrt(a*b); equality when a = b

formulas.name: Profit Maximization via MR = MC
formulas.plain: pi(q) = R(q) - C(q); maximize by d(pi)/dq = 0 => MR = MC; confirm max by d^2(pi)/dq^2 < 0

formulas.name: Equal Cost Sharing with Drop-outs
formulas.plain: n people share cost C; k drop out; extra per person = C/8 - C/10 = C*(1/(n-k) - 1/n); solve for C = extra * n*(n-k)/k

formulas.name: Online Store Price Comparison with Delivery Charge
formulas.plain: Net savings = Price_M - (discount*Price_M + delivery) = (1-discount)*Price_M - delivery; set equal to stated savings

formulas.name: Total Cost (Fixed + Variable) and Average Cost
formulas.plain: Total Cost = FC + VC*Q; Average Cost per unit = FC/Q + VC; if FC = k*Q then avg cost = k + VC (constant)
